description: 1st-century Jewish teacher whose crucifixion and claimed resurrection are foundational to Christianity.
notes: Public ministry c. 27–30; crucifixion under Pontius Pilate (common scholarly dating).

description: Mother of Jesus; present in early Christian memory and tradition.
notes: Dates uncertain; later traditions differ on death/dormition.

apostolic_connection: Connected to Jesus' early life traditions.
description: According to the Gospels, husband of Mary and guardian of Jesus in infancy narratives.

apostolic_connection: Prepared for Jesus' ministry; baptized Jesus.
description: Jewish preacher and baptizer central to the origins of Jesus' movement; executed by Herod Antipas.
notes: Execution traditionally before Jesus' death (c. late 20s/early 30s).

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; leading figure in Jerusalem; associated with Rome in later tradition.
description: Leading apostle in early Christian proclamation; prominent in Acts and early memory.
notes: Death under Nero is traditional; year approximate.

apostolic_connection: Apostle after conversion; worked with Jerusalem leaders; major Gentile missionary.
description: Missionary and letter-writer whose epistles are central to the New Testament and early church expansion.
notes: Martyrdom in Rome is traditional; date approximate.

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; associated with Johannine tradition and later Asia Minor leadership.
description: Apostle traditionally linked to the Fourth Gospel and Johannine letters; later tradition places him at Ephesus.
notes: Death traditionally late 1st century; details disputed.

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; executed in Jerusalem (Acts 12:2).
description: Apostle in Jesus' inner circle; first of the Twelve reported martyred in Acts.
notes: Acts 12:2 is the primary textual anchor for his death.

description: Apostle and early disciple of Jesus.
Apostle mentioned in the Gospels.
Apostle listed among the Twelve; later traditions vary.

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; traditionally linked with the Gospel of Matthew.
description: Apostle traditionally associated with the Gospel of Matthew.
Apostle remembered in the Gospels; later mission traditions vary.
Apostle listed among the Twelve.
Apostle listed among the Twelve; associated with the Epistle of Jude in tradition.
Apostle listed among the Twelve.

apostolic_connection: One of the Twelve; betrayer of Jesus.
description: Apostle known for betraying Jesus in the passion narratives.
Apostle selected after the ascension to replace Judas Iscariot.

name_alt: James of Jerusalem;brother of the Lord
apostolic_connection: Named by Paul as a Jerusalem 'pillar' alongside Peter and John (Gal 2).
description: Leader of the Jerusalem church in the mid-1st century; central to early Gentile inclusion debates.
notes: Death date is approximate; later sources differ on circumstances.

apostolic_connection: One of the Seven appointed in Jerusalem (Acts 6).
description: Deacon and first Christian martyr in Acts.

apostolic_connection: One of the Seven appointed in Jerusalem (Acts 6).
description: Evangelist in Acts; preached in Samaria and baptized the Ethiopian eunuch.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6.
Named among the Seven in Acts 6 as a proselyte from Antioch.

apostolic_connection: Jerusalem disciple; missionary companion of Paul (Acts).
description: Early missionary leader who partnered with Paul and helped integrate converts.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Peter in early tradition; coworker of Paul/Barnabas in Acts.
description: Traditionally linked to the Gospel of Mark and early mission activity.

apostolic_connection: Close coworker of Paul in tradition; attributed author of Luke–Acts.
description: Traditionally attributed author of the Gospel of Luke and Acts of the Apostles.

apostolic_connection: Jerusalem leader and missionary companion of Paul (Acts).
description: Missionary companion of Paul on key journeys recorded in Acts.

apostolic_connection: Coworker and protégé of Paul; recipient in Pastoral Epistles.
description: Paul's close coworker; central figure in Pauline mission and later tradition.

apostolic_connection: Coworker of Paul; associated with organizing churches (e.g., Crete).
description: Pauline coworker connected with mediation and church organization.

apostolic_connection: Alexandrian teacher instructed by Priscilla and Aquila; associated with Corinth (Acts 18).
description: Alexandrian Jewish Christian teacher influential in early churches.

apostolic_connection: Coworker of Paul with Priscilla; hosted house-churches.
description: Jewish Christian coworker of Paul, linked with Priscilla in Acts and letters.

apostolic_connection: Coworker of Paul with Aquila; instructed Apollos.
description: Early Christian teacher and coworker of Paul; often paired with Aquila.
Commended in Romans as a servant/deacon and benefactor of many.
Named by Paul among his kin and fellow prisoners; noted in Rom 16:7.
Named by Paul among his kin and fellow prisoners; noted in Rom 16:7.

apostolic_connection: First major Gentile convert in Acts 10.
description: Roman centurion whose household receives baptism in Acts 10.
Disciple in Damascus who restored Paul's sight and baptized him.
Prophet who foretold a famine and later warned Paul.

apostolic_connection: First convert in Philippi in Acts 16.
description: Merchant in Philippi whose household was baptized; hosted Paul and companions.

apostolic_connection: Disciple in Joppa raised by Peter (Acts 9).
description: Disciple known for charity; raised from death in Acts 9.
Athenian convert named in Acts 17:34.
Athenian woman convert named in Acts 17:34.
Named in Romans 16 as among Paul's acquaintances in Rome.
Named in Romans 16 as someone who worked hard for the community.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 via greetings to his household.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 via greetings to his household.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.

Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Named in Romans 16 among Paul's greetings.
Coworker associated with Colossae; commended in Colossians.
Courier and coworker mentioned in multiple Pauline letters.
Figure in Philemon; named in Colossians as returning with Tychicus.

apostolic_connection: Recipient of Philemon; associated with house church.
description: Recipient of Paul's letter; associated with a house-church.
Named among recipients of Philemon.
Named in Philemon and Colossians; exhorted to fulfill ministry.
Messenger and coworker from Philippi associated with Paul's imprisonment.
Pauline coworker mentioned in Colossians and Philemon; later noted as having deserted in 2 Timothy.
Named in 2 Timothy as having gone to Galatia.
Named in 2 Timothy as holding Paul's cloak and books at Troas.
Named in 2 Timothy among those sending greetings.
Named in 2 Timothy among those sending greetings.
Named in 2 Timothy among those sending greetings.

apostolic_connection: Mentioned in 2 Tim 4:21; later tradition connects him with Rome.
description: Named in 2 Timothy; later tradition lists Linus among early Roman bishops.

apostolic_connection: Witness to crucifixion and resurrection in Gospel narratives.
description: Disciple and prominent resurrection witness in the Gospels.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus' ministry in Bethany.
description: Sister of Martha and Lazarus in the Gospel of John.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus' ministry in Bethany.
description: Sister of Mary and Lazarus in the Gospels.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus' ministry in Bethany (John 11).
description: Figure in John whose raising is central to the narrative.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Jesus in John's Gospel.
description: Pharisee who appears in the Gospel of John; associated with Jesus' burial narrative.

apostolic_connection: Provided tomb for Jesus' burial in Gospel narratives.
description: Figure in passion narratives who secures Jesus' burial.

apostolic_connection: Named among women who supported Jesus (Luke 8:3).
description: Disciple named in Luke among women who supported Jesus' ministry.

apostolic_connection: Named among women who supported Jesus (Luke 8:3).
description: Disciple named in Luke among women who supported Jesus' ministry.
Disciple in the Emmaus road narrative in Luke 24.
Prophetess in Luke who speaks about the child Jesus.
Righteous man in Luke who blesses the infant Jesus and prophesies.
Tax collector in Luke who responds to Jesus and practices restitution.

apostolic_connection: Linked with the Roman church in the late 1st century; associated with 1 Clement.
description: Late-1st-century Roman church leader traditionally associated with the letter known as 1 Clement.
notes: Date and office terminology debated; often treated as an Apostolic Father.

apostolic_connection: Early Roman bishop in traditional succession lists.
description: Figure in early lists of bishops of Rome; chronology and details are uncertain.
notes: Dates and historicity details are debated in scholarship.

apostolic_connection: Early Roman bishop in traditional succession lists (late 1st/early 2nd c).
description: Early bishop of Rome in later episcopal lists; details uncertain.
notes: Often dated late 1st century into early 2nd.

description: Named among prophets and teachers in the Antioch church at the launch of Paul's mission.
Named among prophets and teachers in the Antioch church (Acts 13:1).
Named among prophets and teachers in the Antioch church (Acts 13:1).

apostolic_connection: Jerusalem leader sent with Silas to Antioch (Acts 15:22).
description: Jerusalem leader and prophet sent with the council letter to Antioch.
Candidate proposed to replace Judas Iscariot before Matthias was chosen.

apostolic_connection: Convert in Corinth during Paul's mission (Acts 18:8).
description: Ruler of the synagogue in Corinth who believed with his household.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Corinth and co-sender in 1 Corinthians (1 Cor 1:1).
description: Named in Acts in Corinth and in 1 Corinthians as associated with Paul.

apostolic_connection: Hosted Paul near synagogue in Corinth (Acts 18:7).
description: Worshiper of God in Corinth whose house became a base for Paul's preaching.
Named in Romans among Paul's companions sending greetings.

apostolic_connection: Associated with Thessalonica (Acts 17) and named in Rom 16:21.
description: Host of Paul and companions in Thessalonica; named among Paul's associates.
Scribe who wrote the Epistle to the Romans and adds his greeting.

apostolic_connection: Host mentioned in Rom 16:23; also appears in other NT contexts.
description: Named as host of Paul and the whole church in Romans 16:23.

apostolic_connection: Named in Rom 16:23; also in Acts/letters.
description: Named as city treasurer in Romans 16:23; appears elsewhere in the Pauline circle.
Named in Romans among those sending greetings.
Named in Philippians among two women urged to agree in the Lord.
Named in Philippians among two women urged to agree in the Lord.
Named in Philippians among Paul's coworkers whose names are in the book of life.
Named in 1 Corinthians; his household is described as early converts and devoted to service.
Named in 1 Corinthians among those who visited Paul.
Named in 1 Corinthians among those who visited Paul.
Commended in 2 Timothy for refreshing Paul and searching for him in Rome.

apostolic_connection: Travel companion mentioned in Acts and 2 Tim 4:20.
description: Travel companion associated with Ephesus; mentioned in Acts and 2 Timothy.

apostolic_connection: Travel companion mentioned in Acts and letters.
description: Macedonian travel companion of Paul, mentioned in Acts and Pauline letters.
Listed among Paul's companions on part of the third missionary journey.
Listed among Paul's companions on part of the third missionary journey.
Early disciple who hosted Paul and companions on arrival near Jerusalem.
Young man in Acts 20 who fell from a window and was restored to life.

apostolic_connection: Missionary to Edessa credited with founding Syriac Christianity.
description: Syriac apostle whose mission to Edessa is foundational for the Church of the East; the Doctrine of Addai recounts the mission.
citations: Doctrine of Addai; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 1.13
notes: Possibly same as Thaddaeus; apostolic legend of Edessa.

description: Early bishop of Alexandria (c.167-188) in the Coptic succession list.
First recorded Christian martyr in Britain; sheltered a priest and converted; executed under Diocletian c.304.
Bishop of Rome (c.107-115); associated with early Roman liturgical tradition.
Bishop of Alexandria (312-328) who first condemned Arius and championed the Nicene position; mentor to Athanasius.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 10.5; Theodoret Ecclesiastical History 1

description: Bishop of Milan (374-397); converted from catechumen to bishop in one week; major opponent of Arianism in the West; influenced Augustine.
citations: Augustine Confessions; Paulinus Life of Ambrose
notes: Refused to surrender Milan churches to Empress Justina.

description: Second bishop of Alexandria (c.68-83) according to Coptic tradition.
Bishop of Rome (c.155-166); hosted Polycarp of Smyrna who visited Rome; involved in early Easter dating dispute.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.19; Irenaeus in Eusebius

description: Bishop of Nicomedia martyred at the start of the Diocletianic Great Persecution (303).
Father of Christian monasticism; withdrew to the Egyptian desert c.270; his Life by Athanasius became foundational for monasticism.
citations: Athanasius Life of Anthony; Jerome Lives of the Fathers
notes: Feast day 17 January; died at age c.105.

description: Early martyr at Pergamon mentioned in Revelation 2:13 as a faithful witness killed in the city where Satan dwells.
Bishop of Hierapolis (c.170-180); wrote apologies to Marcus Aurelius and works against Montanism.
Libyan-born priest in Alexandria whose teaching that the Son was a creature not co-eternal with the Father sparked the Arian controversy and the Council of Nicaea.
citations: Athanasius Orations Against the Arians; Council of Nicaea 325
notes: Died suddenly in Constantinople 336, the day before his planned restoration.

description: Bishop of Alexandria (328-373) who championed Nicene homoousios against Arianism; exiled five times; authored Life of Anthony and Easter Festal Letters.
notes: Athanasius contra mundum; Easter Festal Letter 367 first lists the full NT canon.

description: Syriac philosopher and hymnographer; wrote Book of the Laws of Countries; cosmological views later judged heterodox.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 4.30; Book of the Laws of Countries
notes: Father of Syriac hymnody; Ephrem later wrote against his views.

description: Gnostic teacher in Alexandria (c.117-138); founder of the Basilidean school; taught 365 heavens and salvation through gnosis.
Christian slave martyred in the persecution of Lyon and Vienne (177); her endurance became legendary in the early church.
citations: Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne in Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 5.1

description: Bishop of Carthage (c.312-340) whose contested consecration triggered the Donatist schism.
citations: Augustine Against the Donatists; Optatus Against the Donatists
notes: Council of Arles (314) upheld his consecration.

description: Bishop of Rome (c.217-222); formerly a slave; admitted remarried clergy and restored the lapsed; opposed by Hippolytus of Rome.
citations: Hippolytus Refutation of All Heresies 9; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 6
notes: Built the Catacombs of Callixtus on Via Appia.

description: Head of the Alexandrian catechetical school; synthesized Christian faith with Greek philosophy; wrote Protrepticus, Paedagogus, and Stromata.
notes: Teacher of Origen; fled Alexandria during the Severan persecution c.202.

description: Roman Emperor (306-337); issued Edict of Milan (313) granting religious tolerance; convened Council of Nicaea (325); baptized on his deathbed.
notes: First Christian Roman emperor; founded Constantinople.

description: Roman Caesar and Augustus; father of Constantine I; relatively tolerant of Christians in his western territories during the Diocletianic persecution.
citations: Eusebius Life of Constantine; Lactantius De Mortibus Persecutorum
notes: Died at York (Eboracum); Constantine proclaimed emperor by his troops.

description: Bishop of Carthage (249-258); theologian of church unity; executed under Valerian; author of De Unitate Ecclesiae.
Bishop of Jerusalem (350-386); author of the Catechetical Lectures, a primary source for 4th-century baptismal instruction and liturgy; embroiled in Arian controversies and deposed three times.
notes: Doctor of the Church; feast day 18 March.

description: Bishop of Alexandria (248-264); student of Origen; navigated the Decian and Valerian persecutions; wrote on Easter dating and authorship of Revelation.
notes: Called the Great by the Eastern church.

description: Bishop of Rome (260-268); reorganized the Roman church after the Valerian persecution; corresponded with Dionysius of Alexandria on Trinitarian theology.
Leader of the Donatist schism who gave his name to the movement; Bishop of Carthage in the Donatist succession; opposed Caecilian for over four decades.
citations: Augustine Against the Donatists; Optatus Against the Donatists

description: Syriac deacon and hymnographer; fled to Edessa when Nisibis was ceded to Persia (363); wrote against Arianism, Marcionism, and Bardaisanism; Doctor of the Church.
notes: Called the Harp of the Holy Spirit; feast day 9 June.

description: Bishop of Caesarea; author of the Ecclesiastical History, foundational source for early Christian history; adviser to Constantine I.
notes: His Ecclesiastical History covers c.33-324 CE.

description: Arian bishop of Nicomedia (then Beirut and Constantinople); ally of Arius; baptized Constantine I on his deathbed (337); influential at the Arian-favoring imperial court.
citations: Socrates Scholasticus Ecclesiastical History 2; Athanasius Historia Arianorum

description: Bishop of Sebaste (c.357-377); early monastic pioneer in Pontus and Armenia; influenced Basil of Caesarea; condemned at the Council of Gangra (c.340) for extreme asceticism.
citations: Basil of Caesarea letters; Council of Gangra canons

description: Bishop of Rome (236-250); divided Roman church into 7 deaconries; martyred in the Decian persecution (250); Origen wrote a letter to him about his own teachings.
Bishop of Caesarea Cappadocia (c.232-268); student of Origen; ally of Cyprian in the baptism controversy; participated in Paul of Samosata controversy.
Tyrian Christian who became tutor to the Aksumite royal court; consecrated by Athanasius as first bishop of Ethiopia (c.328); evangelized King Ezana.
notes: Called Abba Selama (Father of Peace) in Ethiopia.

description: Cappadocian Father and theologian; briefly Bishop of Constantinople (380-381); presided at the Council of Constantinople (381); his Five Theological Orations are foundational Trinitarian texts.
notes: One of the Three Holy Hierarchs.

description: Bishop of Neocaesarea (c.240-270); student of Origen; credited with miraculous healings; the church he built reportedly grew from 17 to all-but-17-pagans during his tenure.
citations: Gregory of Nyssa Life of Gregory Thaumaturgus; Jerome

description: Bishop of Nazianzus (c.328-374); father of Gregory Nazianzus the Theologian; converted from the Hypsistarian sect.
Apostle of Armenia; converted King Tiridates III (c.301); became first Catholicos of the Armenian Apostolic Church; Armenia is credited as the first nation to adopt Christianity.
notes: Imprisoned for 13 years in a pit before converting the king.

description: Mother of Emperor Constantine I; converted to Christianity; made a pilgrimage to the Holy Land c.326-328 where she founded churches and reportedly discovered the True Cross.
citations: Eusebius Life of Constantine 3.41-47; Socrates Scholasticus

description: Roman theologian and antipope; wrote Refutation of All Heresies and Apostolic Tradition; opponent of popes Callixtus I and Zephyrinus; exiled to Sardinian mines with Pope Pontian where he died.
notes: The Apostolic Tradition attributed to him is a key source for early liturgy.

description: Bishop of Cordoba (c.295-357); principal religious adviser to Constantine I; presided at Nicaea (325); possibly the main drafter of the Nicene Creed.
citations: Eusebius Life of Constantine; Athanasius Historia Arianorum
notes: Signed an Arian formula under pressure at Council of Sirmium (357) aged over 100.

apostolic_connection: Disciple of John the Apostle in later tradition.
description: Bishop of Antioch and early Church Father; wrote seven letters en route to martyrdom in Rome; key witness to early episcopacy and Eucharistic theology.
notes: Letters are primary sources for mono-episcopal church order in the 2nd century.

apostolic_connection: Student of Polycarp of Smyrna who had known the Apostle John.
description: Bishop of Lyon (c.178-202); author of Against Heresies; formulated the apostolic succession and rule of faith arguments against Gnosticism; bridge between the apostolic and post-apostolic generations.
notes: His Against Heresies is the most important anti-gnostic work of the 2nd century.

description: Bishop of Nisibis (c.308-338); attended the Council of Nicaea (325); credited with miraculous defense of Nisibis against Persian sieges; teacher of Ephrem the Syrian.
citations: Theodoret Ecclesiastical History 1.11; Ephrem Hymns on Nisibis

description: Samaritan-born philosopher who converted to Christianity; wrote two Apologies and Dialogue with Trypho; described early Christian worship; executed in Rome under Marcus Aurelius.
notes: His Apologies are the most detailed accounts of early 2nd-century Christian worship and theology.

description: Aksumite king (c.320-360) who converted to Christianity through Frumentius; the first Christian king of Ethiopia; his coins changed from pagan to Christian symbols after conversion.
citations: Rufinus Ecclesiastical History 10.9; Ezana Stone inscriptions
notes: Inscription stones document his conversion and military campaigns.

description: King of Kartli (Iberia) who converted to Christianity c.327 after a miraculous healing attributed to Saint Nino; brought Christianity to Georgia.
notes: His conversion established Christianity as the state religion of Georgia.

description: King of Armenia (c.287-330) who was converted to Christianity by Gregory the Illuminator c.301; Armenia became the first kingdom to adopt Christianity as state religion.
citations: Agathangelos History of Armenia; Faustus of Byzantium
notes: His conversion traditionally dated to 301 CE.

description: Antiochene theologian and biblical scholar; founded influential school of biblical interpretation; teacher of Arius and Eusebius of Nicomedia; martyred under Maximinus Daia.
notes: His Arian-leaning theology influenced his students who formed the Arian party at Nicaea.

description: Shipowner and theologian from Sinope; moved to Rome c.140; excommunicated 144; taught that the OT God was a lesser demiurge distinct from the loving God of Jesus; founded extensive Marcionite network.
citations: Tertullian Against Marcion; Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.27
notes: His canon formation drove proto-orthodox NT canon development.

description: Shipowner and theologian who moved to Rome c.140; excommunicated 144; rejected the Hebrew Bible; compiled an early NT canon; founded extensive Marcionite congregational network.
citations: Tertullian Against Marcion; Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.27
notes: His challenge drove proto-orthodox NT canon formation.

description: Bishop of Lycopolis in Egypt; founder of the Meletian Schism; clashed with Peter of Alexandria over re-admission of the lapsed during the Diocletianic persecution.
citations: Epiphanius Panarion 68; Athanasius Apologia Contra Arianos

description: Bishop of Sardis (c.150-180); apologist; produced the first known list of OT books; wrote a Paschal homily (On Pascha) that is an early example of anti-Jewish rhetoric and high Christology.
notes: On Pascha is an extraordinary example of early Christian liturgical rhetoric.

description: Phrygian prophet who claimed to be the mouthpiece of the Paraclete; founded the New Prophecy (Montanist) movement c.156-172; taught imminent eschatology and strict asceticism.
Bishop of Myra in Lycia (c.317-343); venerated for charitable acts; traditionally attended Nicaea (325); origin of the Santa Claus legend.
notes: Feast day 6 December; relics translated to Bari 1087.

description: Greatest early Christian biblical scholar; wrote Hexapla, Contra Celsum, De Principiis; pupil of Clement; suffered under Decius; later condemned posthumously.
notes: Condemned at 2nd Council of Constantinople (553) for speculative theology.

description: Devoted disciple and biographer of Origen; founded library at Caesarea; teacher of Eusebius; martyred in the Diocletianic persecution.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 7.32; Life of Pamphilus
notes: Eusebius adopted his name (Eusebius Pamphili) in honor.

description: First catholicos of the Church of the East to assert primacy of Seleucia-Ctesiphon; reorganized the Mesopotamian church after Sasanid persecution.
Bishop of Antioch (c.260-272); taught a form of monarchian adoptionism; deposed by the Council of Antioch (268); supported by Queen Zenobia of Palmyra.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 7.27-30; Council of Antioch acts
notes: Council of Nicaea (325) required re-baptism of Paulianists.

description: Early Christian missionary and author of several New Testament epistles who spread Christianity across the Roman Empire.
citations: Acts of the Apostles; Eusebius Ecclesiastical History
notes: Traditionally martyred in Rome under Nero.

description: First major Latin Christian theologian; invented much Latin theological vocabulary (Trinity, Person, Substance); later became a Montanist; wrote Apology, Against Marcion, Against Praxeas.
notes: Called the Father of Latin Christianity; exact death circumstances unknown.

description: Bishop of Antioch (c.169-182); wrote Ad Autolycum, the earliest Christian work to use the word Trinity (triados) in Greek; prominent apologist defending monotheism.
Egyptian-born gnostic teacher who moved to Rome c.136; founded the most influential gnostic school; reportedly almost elected bishop of Rome; authored the Gospel of Truth (possibly).
citations: Irenaeus Against Heresies 1.1-9; Tertullian Against the Valentinians

person_label: veneration of the Forty Martyrs begins

description: Bishop of Rome (189-199); first Latin-speaking pope; attempted to excommunicate Asian churches over the Quartodeciman Easter controversy; first to assert strong Roman episcopal authority.
citations: Eusebius Ecclesiastical History 5.23-25; Irenaeus intervening letter
